title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a woman called Sarah Miles talking about volunteering at a community orchard. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a woman called Sarah Miles talking about volunteering at a community orchard. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A woman called Sarah Miles talks about volunteering at a community orchard.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Sarah Miles, and I coordinate volunteers at Maplewood Community Orchard, a green space just outside the town centre. Visitors often think the orchard has been here for generations because some trees are already quite tall. In fact, the council bought the site only eight years ago. Before that, it had greenhouses and was used as a [9]plant nursery[/9], where local shops bought flowers. Once the owner retired, residents suggested growing fruit there instead of building flats on the land.||We now grow pears, plums and several kinds of apple. People imagine we sell fresh fruit in bags, and we do offer some during the autumn, but that is not our main source of money. We tried producing jam in our first year; it took too long to prepare. These days, [10]apple juice[/10] is the product visitors buy most often at our small weekend stall. It is made locally and the money covers seeds, repairs and new trees. Volunteers can come regularly or just help at special events. Before anyone uses equipment, even simple tools, they must join a [11]safety tour[/11] of the orchard so they know which paths are slippery and where equipment is stored.||There are jobs for people with different levels of experience. Beginners often expect to pick fruit immediately, but that only happens for a short period each year. In spring, [12]watering seedlings[/12] is the task we usually give first-time volunteers, as it is straightforward and lets them learn where everything grows. We provide small tools and sun hats on hot days. However, because the ground remains wet long after rain, I always advise people to bring [13]rubber boots[/13] rather than trainers. The orchard has no mains water near the far beds. Rain from a nearby shelter is collected in a large [14]water tank[/14], which means we can keep young plants healthy without wasting drinking water.||At the end of each morning, volunteers wash tools and return them carefully. We used to leave everything in a wooden shed, but its roof leaked badly one winter. All smaller equipment is now kept in a locked [15]tool cupboard[/15] inside the classroom building, and regular volunteers are shown where to find the key. We also offer monthly talks. A talk on fruit-tree pruning filled almost every seat last year. This year the first session to become fully booked was on [16]beekeeping[/16], perhaps because people want to understand how insects help trees produce fruit.||Many school and college students ask whether volunteering can support future applications. Anyone who attends on six separate occasions is given a [17]certificate[/17], stating which activities they have completed. It is not a qualification, but it shows commitment. Finally, each September we celebrate the end of the fruit-picking season. In previous years we held a small market, but it felt too much like another working day. This autumn we are organising a [18]harvest lunch[/18], with food prepared from local produce. The forms at the back of the room include the dates, and I am happy to answer questions.

dialogue.speaker: sarah
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Sarah Miles, and I coordinate volunteers at Maplewood Community Orchard, a green space just outside the town centre. Visitors often think the orchard has been here for generations because some trees are already quite tall. In fact, the council bought the site only eight years ago. Before that, it had greenhouses and was used as a plant nursery, where local shops bought flowers. Once the owner retired, residents suggested growing fruit there instead of building flats on the land.||We now grow pears, plums and several kinds of apple. People imagine we sell fresh fruit in bags, and we do offer some during the autumn, but that is not our main source of money. We tried producing jam in our first year; it took too long to prepare. These days, apple juice is the product visitors buy most often at our small weekend stall. It is made locally and the money covers seeds, repairs and new trees. Volunteers can come regularly or just help at special events. Before anyone uses equipment, even simple tools, they must join a safety tour of the orchard so they know which paths are slippery and where equipment is stored.||There are jobs for people with different levels of experience. Beginners often expect to pick fruit immediately, but that only happens for a short period each year. In spring, watering seedlings is the task we usually give first-time volunteers, as it is straightforward and lets them learn where everything grows. We provide small tools and sun hats on hot days. However, because the ground remains wet long after rain, I always advise people to bring rubber boots rather than trainers. The orchard has no mains water near the far beds. Rain from a nearby shelter is collected in a large water tank, which means we can keep young plants healthy without wasting drinking water.||At the end of each morning, volunteers wash tools and return them carefully. We used to leave everything in a wooden shed, but its roof leaked badly one winter. All smaller equipment is now kept in a locked tool cupboard inside the classroom building, and regular volunteers are shown where to find the key. We also offer monthly talks. A talk on fruit-tree pruning filled almost every seat last year. This year the first session to become fully booked was on beekeeping, perhaps because people want to understand how insects help trees produce fruit.||Many school and college students ask whether volunteering can support future applications. Anyone who attends on six separate occasions is given a certificate, stating which activities they have completed. It is not a qualification, but it shows commitment. Finally, each September we celebrate the end of the fruit-picking season. In previous years we held a small market, but it felt too much like another working day. This autumn we are organising a harvest lunch, with food prepared from local produce. The forms at the back of the room include the dates, and I am happy to answer questions.

questions.question: Before it became an orchard, the site was used as a (9) .
questions.explanation: Sarah explains that the land had previously been used as a 'plant nursery'.

questions.question: The orchard earns most of its sales income from producing (10) .
questions.explanation: Sarah contrasts jam and fresh fruit with the product that sells best: 'apple juice'.

questions.question: New volunteers must take part in a (11) before using equipment.
questions.explanation: Sarah states that everyone must join a 'safety tour' before using tools.

questions.question: The first job generally given to new volunteers in spring is (12) .
questions.explanation: Sarah says first-time volunteers usually start by 'watering seedlings'.

questions.question: Sarah recommends wearing (13) when the ground is wet.
questions.explanation: She advises volunteers to bring 'rubber boots' rather than trainers.

questions.question: Water used for young plants is stored in a (14) .
questions.explanation: Sarah explains that collected rain is kept in a 'water tank'.

questions.question: Small tools are now stored in a locked (15) .
questions.explanation: The smaller equipment is kept in a locked 'tool cupboard'.

questions.question: This year's first fully-booked talk was about (16) .
questions.explanation: Sarah identifies 'beekeeping' as the first talk to fill up.

questions.question: Volunteers attending six times receive a (17) .
questions.explanation: Sarah says volunteers who attend six occasions are given a 'certificate'.

questions.question: The orchard is holding a (18) at the end of this fruit-picking season.
questions.explanation: Sarah announces a 'harvest lunch' for this autumn.
